title: Trial of Socrates
description: Socrates condemned to death in Athens for impiety and corrupting the youth. Chose hemlock over exile.

title: Epicurus founds the Garden
description: Epicurus establishes his school in Athens, teaching that the gods do not intervene in human affairs and that pleasure (ataraxia) is the highest good.

title: Execution of Michael Servetus
description: Spanish physician and theologian burned at the stake in Geneva for denying the Trinity. Both Catholics and Protestants condemned him.

title: Execution of Giordano Bruno
description: Italian philosopher burned at the stake in Rome for heresy, including his support for Copernican heliocentrism and the idea of infinite worlds.

title: Locke's Letter Concerning Toleration
description: John Locke argues for religious tolerance and the separation of church and state — though he excludes atheists and Catholics.

title: Hume's Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
description: David Hume publishes his devastating critique of miracles and the design argument, establishing empiricism as the foundation of rational inquiry.

title: Voltaire's Treatise on Tolerance
description: Voltaire campaigns for religious tolerance after the judicial murder of Jean Calas, a French Protestant falsely accused of killing his son.

title: Paine's Common Sense
description: Thomas Paine's pamphlet sells 500,000 copies and catalyzes American independence. His democratic radicalism is inseparable from his freethinking.

title: Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom
description: Thomas Jefferson's statute, championed by James Madison, establishes religious freedom in Virginia — a model for the First Amendment.

title: Paine's The Age of Reason
description: Thomas Paine publishes his systematic critique of revealed religion while awaiting execution in a French prison. It costs him his reputation.

title: Darwin's On the Origin of Species
description: Charles Darwin publishes the theory of evolution by natural selection, removing the need for a divine designer.

title: Mill's On Liberty
description: John Stuart Mill publishes the foundational case for free expression and individual autonomy.

title: Ingersoll delivers 'The Gods'
description: Robert G. Ingersoll delivers his first major freethought lecture, launching his career as 'the Great Agnostic' — the most celebrated orator of nineteenth-century America.

title: Elizabeth Cady Stanton's The Woman's Bible
description: Stanton publishes her feminist critique of the Bible, arguing that scripture has been used to subjugate women.

title: Scopes Trial
description: John Scopes convicted for teaching evolution in Tennessee. The trial, featuring Clarence Darrow and William Jennings Bryan, exposes the conflict between science and fundamentalism.

title: Russell's 'Why I Am Not a Christian'
description: Bertrand Russell delivers his famous lecture at Battersea Town Hall, the clearest short statement of secular humanism ever written.

title: Everson v. Board of Education
description: US Supreme Court applies the Establishment Clause to state governments: 'Neither a state nor the federal government can set up a church.'

title: Engel v. Vitale
description: US Supreme Court rules that government-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the First Amendment.

title: Carl Sagan's Cosmos
description: Sagan's television series reaches 500 million viewers worldwide, making science accessible and inspiring a generation of freethinkers.

title: The New Atheism
description: Sam Harris (The End of Faith), Richard Dawkins (The God Delusion), Daniel Dennett (Breaking the Spell), and Christopher Hitchens (God Is Not Great) launch the 'New Atheist' movement.

title: Carvaka School Founded
description: The Carvaka (or Lokayata) school of Indian philosophy rejects the authority of the Vedas, denies the afterlife, and insists that perception is the only valid source of knowledge — one of the earliest materialist traditions.

title: Protagoras and Relativism
description: Protagoras declares 'Man is the measure of all things' and writes 'Concerning the Gods' expressing agnosticism. His books are reportedly burned in Athens.

title: Lucretius' De Rerum Natura
description: The Roman poet Lucretius publishes On the Nature of Things, a six-book poem presenting Epicurean physics and philosophy — arguing that the universe operates through natural causes, not divine intervention.

title: Luther's 95 Theses
description: Martin Luther challenges the Catholic Church's authority, inadvertently opening the door to centuries of religious questioning — though Luther himself would have condemned freethought.

title: Descartes' Discourse on Method
description: René Descartes publishes his method of systematic doubt, establishing reason as the foundation of knowledge with 'I think, therefore I am' — a turning point for rationalist philosophy.

title: Spinoza's Theologico-Political Treatise
description: Baruch Spinoza publishes his radical critique of biblical authorship and argues for freedom of thought and expression — the book is banned across Europe.

title: Matthew Tindal's Christianity as Old as Creation
description: Tindal's work becomes the 'Bible of Deism,' arguing that natural religion is sufficient and that revealed religion adds nothing of value.

title: First Amendment Ratified
description: The First Amendment to the US Constitution is ratified, guaranteeing freedom of religion, speech, and press — establishing the legal foundation for freethought in America.

title: Marx on Religion
description: Karl Marx describes religion as 'the opium of the people' in his Critique of Hegel's Philosophy of Right — a materialist analysis of religion's social function.

title: Harriet Taylor Mill's Enfranchisement of Women
description: Harriet Taylor Mill publishes her argument for women's suffrage and equality, linking the cause of women's rights to the broader struggle for rational, evidence-based governance.

title: Nietzsche Declares God Dead
description: Friedrich Nietzsche's The Gay Science contains the famous passage 'God is dead' — not a celebration but a diagnosis of the crisis facing Western civilization after the collapse of religious certainty.

title: Loi de 1905 in France
description: France passes the law on the separation of Church and State, establishing laïcité — one of the most rigorous church-state separations in the world.

title: First Humanist Manifesto
description: Thirty-four intellectuals sign the Humanist Manifesto, articulating a secular humanist worldview that affirms human agency and rejects supernatural belief.

title: Universal Declaration of Human Rights
description: Article 18 of the UDHR establishes the right to freedom of thought, conscience, and religion — including the right to change religion or belief.

title: Abington v. Schempp
description: The US Supreme Court rules that mandatory Bible readings in public schools violate the Establishment Clause, reinforcing the separation of church and state.

title: Sagan's The Demon-Haunted World
description: Published posthumously in expanded form, Carl Sagan's book becomes the definitive popular defence of scientific thinking and skepticism against pseudoscience and superstition.

title: Rise of the Nones
description: The Pew Research Center reports that one in five Americans has no religious affiliation — the 'Nones' become the fastest-growing demographic in American religious life.

title: Charlie Hebdo Attack
description: Islamist gunmen attack the offices of Charlie Hebdo in Paris, killing 12 people. The attack becomes a global flashpoint for debates about blasphemy, free speech, and religious violence.
